The pointer `%src` does not change within the loop, so the load is uniform. The loop vectorizer should identify this and hoist the load.

The assertion failure suggests that one cost model (VPlan) might be folding/hoisting this uniform load and its users, leading to a lower cost estimate and potentially a different VF choice, while the other (legacy) does not.

This implies that if `BestFactor.Width != LegacyVF.Width`, one of the `planContainsAdditionalSimplifications` calls should return true if the plan for either VF involves simplifications not captured by the legacy cost model.

The issue seems to be that the folding of the uniform load and its dependent instructions is a simplification that the legacy cost model doesn't fully account for, but the VPlan does. However, the `planContainsAdditionalSimplifications` check might not be correctly identifying this situation.

A common pattern for fixing such disagreements is to adjust the conditions under which the assertion is checked, or to improve the `planContainsAdditionalSimplifications` function to correctly identify more cases of VPlan-specific optimizations.

static bool planContainsAdditionalSimplifications(
    VPlanPtr &Plan, LoopVectorizationCostModel::CostContext &CostCtx,
    Loop *OrigLoop) {
  if (!Plan)
    return false;

  // Returns true if any instruction in the VPlan is uniform.
  for (VPInstruction *VPI : Plan->VPInstructions)
    if (VPI->isUniform())
      return true;

  // The legacy cost model does not model the cost of strided windows and
  // the fix-up code for them. If the VPlan contains strided windows, do not
  // assert on cost differences.
  for (auto &Recipe : Plan->getEntry()->getRecipeList())
    if (isa<VPWidenCallRecipe>(&Recipe) &&
        cast<VPWidenCallRecipe>(Recipe).isStridedWindow())
      return true;

}

It's complaining about a disagreement between the VPlan cost model and the legacy cost model when picking the best vectorization factor, or VF.
